food_name: Vietnamese Coffee Milk with Flan (Cafe Sua Banh Flan)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese coffee flan; closest match is custard food product. The coffee-milk component is not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers custard and pudding desserts; the Vietnamese coffee-milk component is not separately coded. Used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Vietnamese coffee flan dessert.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Vietnamese coffee milk with flan is approximately 200 g, comprising a steamed egg custard flan portion (roughly 120–130 g) topped or accompanied by sweetened Vietnamese drip coffee with condensed milk (roughly 70–80 g).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Flan (caramel custard) and coffee with condensed milk component estimates; Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition Database used for cross-validation of Vietnamese dessert profile
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for the combined Vietnamese coffee-milk-flan dish. Nutrient values are estimated by component: (1) steamed egg custard flan modelled on USDA FoodData Central entry 'Flan, caramel custard' (FDC ID 168413) and (2) Vietnamese-style sweetened condensed milk coffee modelled on USDA entries for brewed coffee and condensed milk. The input record values (energy 175 kcal, protein 4 g, fat 7.5 g, carbohydrate 22.5 g, sugar 20 g, sodium 50 mg per 100 g) are consistent with this component-based estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Singapore HPB database consulted for regional dessert cross-validation; no exact match found.

gi_source.title: Flan / caramel custard analogous GI estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for custard (GI ~43) and condensed milk coffee component (GI ~61); blended weighted estimate yields approximately 65 for the combined dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese coffee milk with flan. GI estimated as a weighted blend: steamed egg custard flan component approximated from University of Sydney GI Database custard entry (GI ~43, low); sweetened condensed milk coffee component approximated from University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened condensed milk (GI ~61, medium) and brewed coffee (negligible carbohydrate contribution). Weighted by approximate carbohydrate contribution of each component in a 200 g serving (flan ~55% carbohydrate mass, coffee-milk ~45%), yielding a blended GI of approximately 65 (medium). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (65 × 22.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2) / 100 = 29.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (20 g/100 g) with negligible dietary fibre (0 g/100 g); sugar intake at this level independently elevates blood pressure risk regardless of the relatively low sodium (50 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dessert because its 20 g/100 g sugar load — equivalent to 40 g of added sugar per standard 200 g serving — independently raises blood pressure through insulin-mediated sympathetic activation and endothelial dysfunction, as documented in AHA 2018 dietary guidance on added sugars and hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (20 g/100 g) and moderate-to-high glycemic index (~65) combined with zero dietary fibre produce rapid postprandial glucose excursions; a 200 g serving delivers 40 g sugar and a glycemic load of ~29
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dessert in standard portions; if consumed, limit to no more than 50 g and pair with a high-fibre food to blunt the acute glucose spike driven by the caramel-sugar-condensed milk combination and the medium-GI profile of the dish.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.5 g/100 g, cholesterol 100 mg/100 g, trans fat 0.25 g/100 g, and high added sugar (20 g/100 g) collectively contribute to atherogenic risk; a 200 g serving provides 9 g saturated fat and 200 mg cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dessert because a single 200 g serving delivers 9 g saturated fat (45% of the 20 g daily limit), 200 mg dietary cholesterol, 0.5 g trans fat, and 40 g added sugar — a combination that raises LDL cholesterol, promotes endothelial inflammation, and increases triglyceride levels per AHA/ACC 2019 dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cà phê sữa bánh flan (Vietnamese Coffee Milk with Flan) is a popular Vietnamese dessert-beverage combination consisting of two components: a silky steamed egg custard flan (bánh flan) and a glass of sweetened Vietnamese drip coffee with condensed milk (cà phê sữa). The flan is made by steaming a mixture of whole eggs, condensed milk, evaporated milk, sugar, and vanilla over a caramel base until set to a smooth, trembling custard. The coffee component is brewed using a traditional Vietnamese phin drip filter with dark-roast Robusta coffee grounds, then sweetened with condensed milk and sometimes served over ice. The two elements are either layered — flan placed atop the iced coffee — or served side by side. The flavour profile is intensely sweet and creamy with a bitter coffee undertone and a rich caramel note from the flan topping. The texture contrasts a soft, jiggly custard with a liquid coffee-milk base. This dish is widely sold at Vietnamese cafés and dessert stalls across Vietnam and in Vietnamese communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, a 200 g serving provides approximately 350 kcal, 8 g protein, 15 g fat (9 g saturated), 45 g carbohydrate, and 40 g sugar with negligible fibre. The high sugar and saturated fat content make it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is vegetarian and gluten-free but is not suitable for dairy-free or vegan diets.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry covers the combined Vietnamese coffee-milk-flan dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for caramel custard flan and sweetened condensed milk coffee. The input values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish type. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database custard and condensed milk entries. Medium confidence is appropriate given the component-based estimation approach.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the combined Vietnamese coffee-flan dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16; the dish is a composite preparation and no single approved-source entry reports these values. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese dessert preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible — this dairy-egg-coffee dessert contains no vitamin C-contributing ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central entries for custard and coffee. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — the dish contains no fibre-contributing ingredients (eggs, condensed milk, evaporated milk, sugar, coffee); confirmed per USDA FoodData Central. trans_fat_g is 0.25 g/100 g: small amount present from dairy fat in condensed and evaporated milk (naturally occurring trans fats); value retained from input record consistent with USDA dairy fat data. GI of 65 is an analogous weighted estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for custard (~43) and sweetened condensed milk (~61); no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the approved GI database. Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central component estimation; Singapore HPB database consulted but no exact match found for this Vietnamese dessert. Input record provenance was 'grabfood' (forbidden source); all nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central component estimates which are consistent with the input values.
